hypertension.definition: High blood pressure — a condition where the force of blood against your artery walls is consistently too high, which can lead to heart disease and stroke.

diabetes mellitus.definition: A group of diseases where your body can't properly use sugar (glucose) for energy, causing high blood sugar levels.

diabetes.definition: A condition where your body has trouble controlling blood sugar levels, either because it doesn't make enough insulin or can't use it well.

hyperlipidemia.definition: High levels of fats (cholesterol and triglycerides) in your blood, which can increase your risk of heart disease.

myocardial infarction.definition: A heart attack — when blood flow to part of the heart muscle is blocked, causing damage to the heart.

arrhythmia.definition: An irregular heartbeat — your heart may beat too fast, too slow, or with an uneven rhythm.

atrial fibrillation.definition: An irregular and often rapid heart rhythm starting in the upper chambers of the heart, which can increase stroke risk.

fibrillation.definition: Rapid, irregular twitching of heart muscle fibers that prevents the heart from pumping blood effectively.

thrombosis.definition: A blood clot that forms inside a blood vessel, which can block blood flow and cause serious problems.

embolism.definition: A blockage in a blood vessel caused by a blood clot or other material that has traveled from another part of the body.

pulmonary embolism.definition: A blood clot in the lungs that blocks blood flow, causing difficulty breathing and can be life-threatening.

edema.definition: Swelling caused by excess fluid trapped in your body's tissues, often in the legs, ankles, or feet.

dyspnea.definition: Shortness of breath or difficulty breathing, which can range from mild to severe.

tachycardia.definition: A faster than normal heart rate (over 100 beats per minute) while at rest.

bradycardia.definition: A slower than normal heart rate (under 60 beats per minute), which may not pump enough blood to the body.

angina.definition: Chest pain or pressure caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, often a sign of heart disease.

carcinoma.definition: A type of cancer that starts in cells that line the inner or outer surfaces of the body (like skin or organ linings).

lymphoma.definition: A cancer that begins in the lymphatic system (the body's disease-fighting network), affecting white blood cells called lymphocytes.

sarcoma.definition: A rare type of cancer that develops in bones, muscles, fat, blood vessels, or other connective tissues.

melanoma.definition: The most serious type of skin cancer, which develops in the cells that give your skin its color.

metastasis.definition: The spread of cancer from where it started to other parts of the body through the blood or lymph system.

benign.definition: Not cancerous — a growth that does not spread to other parts of the body.

malignant.definition: Cancerous — cells that can invade nearby tissues and spread to other parts of the body.

pneumonia.definition: An infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, which may fill with fluid, causing cough and difficulty breathing.

bronchitis.definition: Inflammation of the lining of the bronchial tubes (airways), causing coughing and mucus production.

asthma.definition: A condition where your airways narrow and swell, producing extra mucus that makes breathing difficult.

copd.definition: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease — a group of lung diseases (including emphysema and chronic bronchitis) that block airflow and make it hard to breathe.

emphysema.definition: A lung condition where the air sacs are damaged, making it hard to breathe out, often caused by smoking.

anemia.definition: A condition where you don't have enough healthy red blood cells to carry adequate oxygen to your body's tissues, causing fatigue.

hemoglobin.definition: The protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from your lungs to the rest of your body.

platelet.definition: Tiny blood cells that help your body form clots to stop bleeding.

leukocyte.definition: White blood cells — part of your immune system that helps fight infections and diseases.

erythrocyte.definition: Red blood cells — cells that carry oxygen from your lungs to all parts of your body.

creatinine.definition: A waste product from normal muscle activity that your kidneys filter out. High levels may indicate kidney problems.

bilirubin.definition: A yellow substance made when your body breaks down old red blood cells. High levels can cause jaundice (yellowing of skin).

troponin.definition: A protein released into the blood when the heart muscle is damaged. Elevated levels often indicate a heart attack.

albumin.definition: A protein made by your liver that keeps fluid in your bloodstream. Low levels can indicate liver or kidney disease.

hepatitis.definition: Inflammation of the liver, usually caused by a viral infection, which can impair the liver's ability to function.

cirrhosis.definition: Late-stage scarring of the liver caused by long-term liver damage, which prevents the liver from working properly.

nephritis.definition: Inflammation of the kidneys that can affect how well they filter waste from the blood.

arthritis.definition: Inflammation of one or more joints, causing pain, swelling, and stiffness that can worsen with age.

osteoporosis.definition: A condition where bones become weak and brittle, making them more likely to break.

fracture.definition: A broken bone, which can range from a thin crack to a complete break.

appendicitis.definition: A painful swelling of the appendix (a small pouch connected to the large intestine) that usually requires surgery.

cholecystitis.definition: Inflammation of the gallbladder, usually caused by gallstones blocking the tube leading out of the gallbladder.

pancreatitis.definition: Inflammation of the pancreas, which can cause severe abdominal pain, nausea, and digestive problems.

gastritis.definition: Inflammation of the stomach lining that can cause pain, nausea, and indigestion.

colitis.definition: Inflammation of the inner lining of the colon (large intestine), causing diarrhea, pain, and bloating.

diverticulitis.definition: Inflammation or infection of small pouches (diverticula) that form in the walls of the intestine.

thyroid.definition: A butterfly-shaped gland in your neck that makes hormones controlling how your body uses energy.

hypothyroidism.definition: An underactive thyroid gland — when your thyroid doesn't make enough hormones, causing tiredness, weight gain, and feeling cold.

hyperthyroidism.definition: An overactive thyroid gland — when your thyroid makes too much hormone, causing weight loss, rapid heartbeat, and nervousness.

levothyroxine.definition: A medication used to treat hypothyroidism. It replaces the thyroid hormone your body isn't making enough of.

metformin.definition: A medication used to treat type 2 diabetes by helping control the amount of sugar in your blood.

lisinopril.definition: A blood pressure medication (ACE inhibitor) used to treat high blood pressure and heart failure.

atorvastatin.definition: A cholesterol-lowering medication (statin) that reduces 'bad' cholesterol and fats in the blood.

omeprazole.definition: A medication that reduces stomach acid production, used to treat heartburn, acid reflux, and stomach ulcers.

amoxicillin.definition: An antibiotic used to treat a wide variety of bacterial infections by stopping the growth of bacteria.

azithromycin.definition: An antibiotic used to treat many types of bacterial infections, including respiratory and skin infections.

acetaminophen.definition: A common pain reliever and fever reducer (brand name Tylenol) used to treat mild to moderate pain.

ibuprofen.definition: An anti-inflammatory pain reliever (brand names Advil, Motrin) used to reduce fever, pain, and swelling.

hydrochlorothiazide.definition: A water pill (diuretic) that helps your body get rid of extra salt and water, used to treat high blood pressure.

prognosis.definition: The likely course and expected outcome of a disease — what doctors think will happen with the condition over time.

diagnosis.definition: The identification of a disease or condition based on your symptoms, medical history, and test results.

etiology.definition: The cause or origin of a disease — what led to the condition developing.

pathology.definition: The study of disease — examining tissues and body fluids to diagnose illness.

biopsy.definition: A procedure where a small sample of tissue is removed from your body and examined under a microscope to check for disease.

mri.definition: Magnetic Resonance Imaging — a scan that uses strong magnets and radio waves to create detailed pictures of the inside of your body.

ct scan.definition: Computed Tomography scan — a special X-ray that takes detailed cross-section pictures of the inside of your body.

ultrasound.definition: An imaging test that uses sound waves to create pictures of structures inside your body.

echocardiogram.definition: An ultrasound of the heart that shows how well your heart is pumping blood and whether the valves are working properly.

electrocardiogram.definition: A test (also called ECG or EKG) that records the electrical activity of your heart to detect problems with heart rhythm.

intravenous.definition: Given through a vein — a way to deliver fluids or medication directly into your bloodstream through a small tube in a vein.

subcutaneous.definition: Under the skin — a way to give injections into the fatty tissue just below the skin surface.

intramuscular.definition: Into the muscle — a way to give injections deep into a muscle so the medication is absorbed into the bloodstream.

chronic.definition: Long-lasting — a condition that persists for a long time (usually more than 3 months) or keeps coming back.

acute.definition: Short-term but often severe — a condition that starts suddenly and usually lasts a short time.

bilateral.definition: On both sides — affecting both the left and right sides of the body.

anterior.definition: The front — referring to the front part of the body or an organ.

posterior.definition: The back — referring to the back part of the body or an organ.

contraindicated.definition: Not recommended — a treatment or medication that should not be used because it could be harmful in your situation.

sepsis.definition: A life-threatening condition where your body's response to an infection damages its own tissues and organs.

ischemia.definition: Reduced blood flow to a part of the body, which means that area isn't getting enough oxygen.

stenosis.definition: Abnormal narrowing of a passage in the body, such as a blood vessel or spinal canal.

aneurysm.definition: A bulge or ballooning in a weakened area of a blood vessel wall, which can be dangerous if it ruptures.

hematoma.definition: A collection of blood outside the blood vessels, usually caused by an injury — similar to a deep bruise.

abscess.definition: A pocket of pus that forms when your body is fighting an infection, causing swelling and pain.

lesion.definition: An area of abnormal tissue in the body, which can be caused by injury, disease, or other conditions.

neoplasm.definition: An abnormal growth of tissue — commonly called a tumor, which may be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer).

remission.definition: A decrease or disappearance of signs and symptoms of a disease, especially cancer.

relapse.definition: When a disease returns after a period of improvement or remission.

prophylaxis.definition: Preventive treatment — actions taken to prevent a disease from occurring or spreading.

palliative.definition: Treatment focused on providing relief from symptoms and improving quality of life, rather than curing the disease.

comorbidity.definition: Having two or more medical conditions at the same time — for example, having both diabetes and high blood pressure.

immunodeficiency.definition: A weakened immune system that makes it harder for your body to fight infections and diseases.

anticoagulant.definition: A blood thinner — medication that helps prevent blood clots from forming or getting bigger.

analgesic.definition: A pain reliever — any medicine used to reduce or eliminate pain.

antipyretic.definition: A fever reducer — medication that lowers body temperature when you have a fever.

antibiotic.definition: A medicine that kills bacteria or stops them from growing, used to treat bacterial infections.

antihypertensive.definition: A medication used to lower high blood pressure.

diuretic.definition: A water pill — medication that helps your kidneys remove extra water and salt from your body through urine.

insulin.definition: A hormone made by the pancreas that helps sugar enter cells for energy. People with diabetes may need insulin injections.

glucose.definition: Blood sugar — the main type of sugar in your blood that your body uses for energy.

cholesterol.definition: A waxy, fat-like substance in your blood. Your body needs some cholesterol, but too much can build up in your arteries.

triglyceride.definition: A type of fat found in your blood. High levels can increase your risk of heart disease.

hba1c.definition: A blood test that shows your average blood sugar level over the past 2-3 months, used to monitor diabetes control.

electrolyte.definition: Minerals in your blood (like sodium, potassium, calcium) that carry electrical charges and help your body function properly.

potassium.definition: A mineral that helps your heart, muscles, and nerves work properly. Abnormal levels can be dangerous.

sodium.definition: A mineral (salt) that helps control fluid balance and blood pressure in your body.
